source.module: Core Django Fundamentals – Templates & Static Content

topicTags: Core Django Fundamentals – Templates & Static Content

content.problemStatement:
In Django templates, conditionals allow you to control the flow of your template rendering based on certain conditions. \
This is similar to how you use if statements in Python views, but with a slightly different syntax for templates. \
Django templates support three main conditional tags: 
`{% if %}, {% else %}` and `{% elif %}`. 

**Here's how you can use conditionals in your Django templates:**

1. `{% if %}` tag: \
   Use this to check if a condition is true. If it is, the content inside the if block will be rendered.

   Example:
```python
   {% if user.is_authenticated %}
       Welcome, {{ user.username }}!
   {% endif %}
```

2. `{% else %}` tag: \
   This is used in conjunction with {% if %} to provide an alternative when the condition is false.

   Example:
```python
   {% if user.is_authenticated %}
       Welcome, {{ user.username }}!
   {% else %}
       Please log in to continue.
   {% endif %}
```

3. `{% elif %}` tag: \
   Short for "else if", this tag allows you to check multiple conditions in sequence.

   Example:
```python
   {% if user.is_superuser %}
       Welcome, admin!
   {% elif user.is_authenticated %}
       Welcome, {{ user.username }}!
   {% else %}
       Please log in to continue.
   {% endif %}
```

You can also use comparison operators in your conditions, such as ==, !=, , =. Boolean operators 'and', 'or', and 'not' are also supported.

Example:
```python
{% if user.age >= 18 and user.is_verified %}
    You can create an email account.
{% endif %}
```

Remember that these conditionals work with the context data you pass from your views. \
For instance, if you have a view that passes a 'user' object to the template, you can use its attributes in your conditionals.
